title: Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center Unauthenticated RCE CVE-2026-20131 (CVSS 10.0, CISA KEV): Insecure Java Deserialization Grants Root Access — Interlock Ransomware Exploited as Zero-Day 36 Days Before Disclosure, Entire Managed Firewall Estate at Risk
affected_products: Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center (FMC) 6.x (all versions)
Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center (FMC) 7.0.x prior to 7.0.6.3
Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center (FMC) 7.2.x prior to 7.2.5.1
Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center (FMC) 7.4.x prior to 7.4.2.1

timeline.event: Interlock ransomware group begins zero-day exploitation of CVE-2026-20131 against enterprise firewall management systems — detected retroactively via Amazon MadPot honeypot network
Cisco publicly discloses CVE-2026-20131 and CVE-2026-20079 and releases security patches after 36+ days of zero-day exploitation in the wild
Cisco updates security advisory confirming active in-the-wild exploitation of the vulnerability
CISA adds CVE-2026-20131 to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog with emergency federal remediation deadline of March 22, 2026
Federal civilian executive branch agency remediation deadline expires — agencies that have not patched are in non-compliance with CISA BOD 22-01
Vulnerability remains actively exploitable on unpatched FMC instances; Interlock ransomware campaign ongoing

simple_summary.what_happened: A maximum-severity vulnerability (CVSS 10.0) was discovered in Cisco's Firewall Management Center (FMC) — the central brain used by organizations to manage their entire fleet of Cisco firewalls. The flaw exists in how FMC processes Java data sent to its web interface: any attacker who can reach the management interface can send a specially crafted request that tricks the server into executing arbitrary code as root, with no authentication required. Critically, the Interlock ransomware group was already exploiting this vulnerability as a zero-day for 36 days before Cisco even released a patch. CISA issued an emergency directive requiring all US federal agencies to patch by March 22, 2026.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Any organization running Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center (FMC) on-premises, particularly versions 6.x (all), 7.0.x prior to 7.0.6.3, 7.2.x prior to 7.2.5.1, and 7.4.x prior to 7.4.2.1. Cloud-Delivered FMC is not affected. This is especially critical for organizations where FMC management interfaces are accessible from compromised internal networks, not just the internet — even internal exposure creates exploitable conditions given ransomware's lateral movement capabilities.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: Attackers identify Cisco FMC instances accessible via the network — either internet-facing instances or reachable via compromised internal systems. Step 2: Attackers generate a malicious serialized Java object using exploitation frameworks, crafting a payload that exploits vulnerable Java library gadget chains (such as CommonsCollections) present in the FMC application. Step 3: Attackers send an unauthenticated HTTP POST request to the FMC web management interface containing the malicious serialized payload in the request body. Step 4: The FMC application deserializes the untrusted Java object without any validation — triggering execution of the embedded gadget chain, which runs arbitrary Java code with root privileges. Step 5: Attackers use the root-level code execution to deploy fileless web shells (stored entirely in memory, no disk artifacts), download and execute ransomware binaries, and install ConnectWise ScreenConnect for persistent remote access. Step 6: Attackers leverage FMC's management plane to modify firewall policies across the entire managed device estate — potentially disabling security rules, creating backdoor access rules, or causing widespread network disruption. Step 7: Interlock ransomware performs reconnaissance via PowerShell scripts, stages stolen data in ZIP archives on network shares, and ultimately encrypts critical systems.
what_you_should_do: Apply Cisco patches immediately: update FMC to version 7.0.6.3, 7.2.5.1, or 7.4.2.1 — no workaround exists, patching is the only remediation
Restrict FMC management interface access to trusted management networks only — if your FMC is accessible from the internet, block it at the firewall immediately as an emergency measure

Conduct a forensic investigation going back to January 26, 2026 — look for evidence of Interlock ransomware activity including unusual firewall policy changes, unexpected network connections from FMC, and unauthorized remote access tool (ScreenConnect) installations
Search for fileless webshell activity and ConnectWise ScreenConnect installations on FMC systems and any servers that may have been reached via lateral movement from a compromised FMC
Review all Cisco firewall policy changes made since January 2026 for unauthorized modifications — compromised FMC could have been used to weaken firewall rules across the entire estate
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: A CVSS 10.0 vulnerability means perfection in attacker advantage — no credentials, no user interaction, remotely exploitable, and grants the highest privilege level (root) on a device that controls all your firewalls. Compromising the FMC is not just compromising one server — it means an attacker controls your entire firewall estate, can read all network traffic, disable security rules, and pivot to every network segment. The 36-day zero-day window means any organization that had not patched by March 4 may already be compromised. Interlock ransomware is known for both data encryption and double extortion (threatening to publish stolen data). Organizations should treat any unpatched FMC as compromised and conduct full forensic investigation.

tactics.description: Attackers exploit CVE-2026-20131 via unauthenticated HTTP request to the FMC web management interface, triggering insecure Java deserialization for root-level code execution without any credentials
Post-exploitation PowerShell scripts deployed on victim Windows systems perform comprehensive reconnaissance: enumerating processes, installed software, running services, and collecting browser data
Interlock deploys fileless web shells on FMC systems that execute entirely in memory, leaving minimal disk artifacts while maintaining persistent access through server reboots
Fileless web shells execute in memory without writing to disk, evading endpoint detection tools that rely on file-based scanning and analysis
PowerShell reconnaissance scripts enumerate user accounts, installed software, running services, and active network connections to map the victim environment for subsequent ransomware deployment
Compromised FMC management plane is leveraged to access firewall policies controlling network segmentation, facilitating lateral movement to adjacent network segments and managed Firepower devices

Stolen data is organized into per-host directories, compressed into ZIP archives on network shares, and exfiltrated via HTTP PUT requests to Interlock's command-and-control infrastructure for double-extortion leverage
Interlock ransomware encrypts critical organizational systems following data exfiltration, demanding ransom payment with threat to publish stolen data if not paid

detection_guidance.summary: Detection for CVE-2026-20131 exploitation requires monitoring at multiple layers. Network-based detection should focus on unusual HTTP traffic with large binary payloads to FMC management interfaces and HTTP PUT requests exfiltrating data from FMC. Host-based detection should look for unexpected process spawning from FMC Java processes, fileless webshell indicators, and unauthorized ConnectWise ScreenConnect installations. Given the zero-day exploitation window starting January 26, 2026, retroactive log analysis back to that date is essential for all FMC deployments.

log_sources.source: FMC System Logs and Audit Trail
log_sources.description: Review FMC-specific logs for unexpected code execution, unauthorized administrative actions, and policy modifications with no associated admin account — root-level exploitation may appear as system-initiated changes
Monitor for HTTP requests containing Java serialized object magic bytes (0xACED 0x0005) in request bodies to FMC management ports — definitive indicator of Java deserialization exploitation attempt
Monitor for unexpected outbound HTTP PUT requests originating from the FMC server, and unusual connections from FMC to external IP addresses — indicators of active data exfiltration

log_sources.source: Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) on FMC Server
log_sources.description: Monitor for Java process spawning system commands (cmd, bash, sh), unexpected network connections from the FMC Java process, and ConnectWise ScreenConnect installation events

log_sources.source: Windows Security Event Logs (for downstream victims)
log_sources.description: PowerShell script block logging (Event ID 4104) reveals Interlock's reconnaissance scripts; Event ID 4688 process creation shows zip archive staging and data preparation activity

what_to_look_for.indicator: Java serialized object in HTTP request to FMC management interface
what_to_look_for.description: HTTP POST requests containing Java serialized magic bytes (0xACED) in the request body to FMC web management endpoints are the fingerprint of CVE-2026-20131 exploitation

what_to_look_for.indicator: HTTP PUT requests from FMC to external destinations
what_to_look_for.description: Outbound HTTP PUT requests from the FMC server to external IP addresses indicate active data exfiltration — Interlock ransomware uses PUT to stage stolen data on attacker-controlled servers
what_to_look_for.log_field: src=FMC_IP AND method='PUT' AND dst NOT IN trusted_cisco_destinations

what_to_look_for.description: Changes to firewall rules, access control policies, or security zone configurations that cannot be attributed to a specific administrative action are a high-fidelity indicator of attacker manipulation of the management plane

what_to_look_for.indicator: ConnectWise ScreenConnect installation on FMC or related systems
what_to_look_for.description: Unauthorized installation of ConnectWise ScreenConnect (remote desktop tool) is a specific Interlock ransomware TTPs for establishing persistent access after initial FMC compromise
what_to_look_for.log_field: Image LIKE '%ScreenConnect%' AND Initiator NOT IN authorized_admins

immediate_actions.description: Update to FMC 7.0.6.3, 7.2.5.1, or 7.4.2.1. No workarounds exist — patching is the only effective remediation. CISA's mandatory federal deadline of March 22 has passed; any unpatched FMC is in active violation and at severe risk.

immediate_actions.action: Restrict FMC management interface network access
immediate_actions.description: Immediately block all access to the FMC web management interface from untrusted networks, including the internet. FMC should only be reachable from dedicated, segmented management networks with multi-factor authentication enforced at all entry points.

immediate_actions.action: Conduct retroactive forensic investigation from January 26, 2026
immediate_actions.description: The zero-day exploitation window began January 26, 2026. Analyze FMC logs, network traffic, and downstream firewall policy changes from that date forward. Treat any FMC that was network-accessible on or after January 26 as potentially compromised until investigation proves otherwise.

immediate_actions.action: Hunt for Interlock ransomware artifacts on FMC and connected systems
immediate_actions.description: Search for ConnectWise ScreenConnect installations, fileless webshell activity, PowerShell reconnaissance script execution, and ZIP archives on network shares containing staged data. These are specific Interlock post-exploitation artifacts.

immediate_actions.action: Review all managed firewall policy changes since January 2026
immediate_actions.description: Audit every Cisco FTD firewall policy change made since January 26, 2026 for unauthorized modifications — disabled security rules, new permit rules to attacker infrastructure, or changes to network segmentation. Attackers with FMC access can silently modify firewall policies across your entire estate.
